label: NHS-Galleri MCED Trial

sources.name: NHS-Galleri trial (ISRCTN91431511)
sources.note: Randomized controlled trial of the Galleri methylation-based multi-cancer early detection (MCED) blood test in a general NHS screening population. Use for endpoint, population, enrollment size, and high-level design facts.

sources.name: PATHFINDER study (GRAIL)
sources.note: Prospective MCED study reporting cancer signal detection and positive predictive value in a screening-eligible population. Use for PPV / diagnostic-yield context.

sources.name: Stewart & Barr, 'The Grail Trial Didn't Fail. It Asked the Wrong Question.' (2026)
sources.note: Argues the composite all-cancer late-stage endpoint was the wrong question for a low-prevalence screening population, and that a pre-specified high-mortality subgroup did show stage shift.

knownFacts: Population: adults aged 50-77 from a general NHS screening population, ~140,000 participants randomized.
Primary endpoint: reduction in the combined (composite) incidence of Stage III and Stage IV cancer diagnoses across ALL cancer types.
The composite all-cancer endpoint was not met at the pre-specified interim analysis.
A pre-specified subgroup of ~12 high-mortality cancers (including pancreatic, ovarian, liver, lung, esophageal, stomach) showed a more substantial late-stage reduction signal.
Concern: in a general, average-risk screening population the prevalence of any single cancer is low, which lowers the positive predictive value (PPV) of a positive test.
quantContext: MCED PPV in screening-eligible populations has been reported in the ~40-43% range (PATHFINDER), but pooled across a general low-prevalence population the realized PPV for any specific late-stage shift is diluted.

A broad composite endpoint pools high-incidence, slow-shifting cancers (e.g. prostate, breast already covered by existing screening) with rare high-mortality cancers, diluting the measurable stage-shift effect size.
Powering an all-cancer composite for stage shift requires either a very large effect or a very large/long trial; enrichment for high-mortality cancers raises the per-event signal.
demoCaveats: Do not present the inferred high-mortality subgroup strategy as a verified clinical recommendation — it is a design hypothesis.
Label any specific numeric PPV, NNT, or effect-size figure as an estimate or assumption unless it is in the known facts.
Exact enrollment and interim-analysis numbers should be treated as approximate context, not audited trial data.
